section.title: David Established in Jerusalem
section.sectionRationale: Set between the failed first attempt to bring the ark, chapter 13, and the corrected procession, chapter 15, this chapter pauses to show David's kingdom taking visible shape: Hiram of Tyre builds him a palace, his household grows, and, most importantly, David twice defeats the Philistines by directly inquiring of God rather than presuming on past victories. The chapter models dependent kingship, deliberately contrasted with Saul, who famously 'did not inquire of the LORD,' 10:14.
section.thesis: The LORD Himself establishes and exalts David's kingdom, and David's greatness is shown not in self-reliance but in repeatedly stopping to inquire of God before acting, a pattern of dependence that stands in sharp contrast to Saul's failure and models the kind of king, and the kind of people, God's covenant community is meant to be.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Hiram's gift and David's growing house, verses 1 to 7; the first Philistine victory at Baal-perazim after David inquires of God, verses 8 to 12; and the second victory after David inquires again and obeys God's precise battle plan, verses 13 to 17.

units.label: Hiram's gift and David's growing house
units.note: the LORD had established him as king

verses.text: Now Hiram king of Tyre sent envoys to David, along with cedar logs, stonemasons, and carpenters, to build a {{bayit|palace}} for him.
And David realized that the LORD had established him as king over Israel and had highly {{nasa|exalted}} his kingdom for the sake of His people Israel.
And David took more wives in Jerusalem and became the father of more sons and daughters.
These are the names of the children born to him in Jerusalem: Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon,

units.label: The first victory: David inquires of God
units.note: should I go up against the Philistines

verses.text: When the Philistines heard that David had been anointed king over all Israel, they all went in search of him; but David learned of this and went out to face them.
Now the Philistines had come and raided the Valley of Rephaim.
So David {{darash|inquired}} of God, “Should I go up against the Philistines? Will You deliver them into my hand?” “Go,” replied the LORD, “for I will deliver them into your hand.”

So David and his men went up to Baal-perazim, where he defeated the Philistines and said, “Like a bursting flood, God has burst out against my enemies by my hand.” So they called that place Baal-perazim.
There the Philistines abandoned their gods, and David ordered that they be burned in the fire.

units.label: The second victory: David inquires again
units.note: David did as God had commanded him

verses.text: Once again the Philistines raided the valley.
So David again {{darash|inquired}} of God, who answered him, “Do not march up after them, but circle around them and attack them in front of the balsam trees.
As soon as you hear the sound of marching in the tops of the balsam trees, move out to battle, because this will mean that God has gone out before you to strike the camp of the Philistines.”
So David did as God had commanded him, and they struck down the army of the Philistines all the way from Gibeon to Gezer.
And David's fame went out into every land, and the LORD caused all nations to fear him.

bayit.gloss: House or, here, palace. Hiram's craftsmen build David a bayit of cedar in Jerusalem. The same word will govern the next several chapters: David wants to build the LORD a bayit, and the LORD instead promises to build David a bayit, a dynasty, 17:1-14.

nasa.gloss: To lift up, carry, bear, or exalt. Here it describes how the LORD 'highly exalted' David's kingdom, literally lifted it up. The same root describes the Levites carrying the ark on their shoulders in chapter 15; whether lifting up a king or bearing up God's presence, the word points to what only God can rightly raise or what only His appointed servants may rightly bear.

darash.gloss: To inquire, seek, or consult. David inquires of God twice in this chapter before going to battle, the very thing Saul famously failed to do, 10:14. The Chronicler consistently measures Israel's kings by whether they darash the LORD before acting.

basic.context: This chapter sits between the failed and corrected attempts to bring home the ark, chapters 13 and 15, and pauses to show David's kingdom taking shape under God's hand. Hiram, king of the great trading city of Tyre, sends cedar and craftsmen to build David a palace, a mark of international recognition. David's family grows in Jerusalem. Then the Philistines, who had tolerated Saul, move immediately against David once he is anointed over all Israel, raiding the Valley of Rephaim near Jerusalem. Twice David is attacked, and twice, rather than assuming he knows what to do, he stops and asks God directly whether and how to fight. Both times God answers, and both times David wins decisively, his fame spreading through every land.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative that doubles as a character study in dependent leadership. The chapter's key phrase, 'David inquired of God' (verse 10) and 'David again inquired of God' (verse 14), is placed by the Chronicler in deliberate contrast to the summary verdict on Saul: he died because he was unfaithful to the LORD and 'did not inquire of the LORD,' 10:13-14. The lesson is not a formula for guaranteed victory but a picture of a king, and a people, whose strength lies in humble dependence on God's direction rather than in past experience or self-confidence.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter's structure is built on two Hebrew roots. Nasa describes how the LORD 'highly exalted' David's kingdom, lifted it up, the same root that will describe the Levites lifting the ark on their shoulders in chapter 15; David's exaltation and the ark's proper handling are both, at root, about what God alone raises up or authorizes. And darash, to inquire or seek, appears twice as David asks God directly before each battle, the very verb whose absence condemned Saul, 10:14.
basic.typology: David inquiring of God before every battle, and receiving precise, situation-specific guidance, foreshadows the perfect, unbroken dependence of great David's greater Son, who said, 'I can do nothing on My own... I do not seek My own will but the will of Him who sent Me,' John 5:30. Where David's dependence was faithful but partial, Christ's was total.
basic.christ: David's pattern of inquiring of God rather than presuming on his own strength or past victories models the very dependence perfectly fulfilled in Jesus, who did nothing apart from the Father, John 5:19, 30. The kingdom that the LORD 'establishes' and 'exalts' for David here, verse 2, anticipates the greater exaltation of Christ, whom God has highly exalted and given the name above every name, Philippians 2:9, the true King before whom every enemy will finally be subdued.
basic.god: God is the one who actually establishes and exalts David's kingdom, 'for the sake of His people Israel,' not for David's own glory. He is also personally engaged and specific in guidance, not a distant force: He answers David's inquiries directly, with tactical detail, both permitting and directing the battle. God's aim throughout is His people's good and His own name being feared among the nations.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Chronicles 10:13-14 states that Saul died because he did not inquire of the LORD, the deliberate contrast this chapter sets up. 2 Samuel 5:17-25 records the same two Philistine battles in near-identical detail. John 5:19, 30 shows Christ's own total dependence on the Father. Philippians 2:9-11 speaks of God highly exalting Jesus, echoing the language of David's exaltation here.
basic.oneStory: Set between two ark narratives, this chapter shows the LORD building David's house and fame while David models the humble dependence, inquiring of God rather than presuming, that Saul so fatally lacked. It is a study in what faithful kingship looks like, pointing forward to the true King who never acted apart from His Father's will and whom God has now highly exalted above all.

context.literary: The Chronicler deliberately interrupts the ark narrative (chapters 13 and 15) with this chapter of political and military consolidation, which mirrors 2 Samuel 5:11-25 almost verse for verse but is repositioned here to sit between the ark's failed and corrected journeys. The effect is to frame David's external successes as flowing directly from, and confirming, his pursuit of God, even while the more important matter of the ark's homecoming still awaits correction.
context.historical: Hiram of Tyre ruled a wealthy Phoenician trading city famed for cedar timber and skilled craftsmen; his alliance with David, later renewed for Solomon's temple, 2 Chronicles 2:3, signaled David's arrival as a recognized regional power. The Valley of Rephaim lay just southwest of Jerusalem, making the Philistine raids a direct threat to David's new capital, not a distant border skirmish.
context.canonical: For the post-exilic community, without a king, a palace, or full security, this chapter's picture of a divinely established, divinely guided king answered real longing: it showed that Israel's true security had always come not from David's own cunning but from a king who sought the LORD, a pattern of hope now invested in the Davidic line's ultimate heir.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that David's growing house, fame, and military success flow from the LORD's own establishing hand and from David's consistent practice of inquiring of God, in pointed contrast to Saul's fatal failure to do the same, 10:13-14.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes David's practice and its outcomes; it does not prescribe that inquiring of God guarantees a particular battle plan or outcome for every believer today. What it does teach as an abiding pattern is the posture of humble dependence on God rather than self-reliance or presumption, especially when facing what looks like a repeat of a past situation.

debates.question: Why did David inquire of God separately for what looks like the same battle twice?

views.label: Because circumstances and God's plan had genuinely changed
views.case: The second Philistine raid, though in the same valley, required a different tactic, circling around and attacking from the balsam trees rather than a direct advance, showing that God's specific guidance for one situation should never be presumed to apply automatically to the next, even a similar one.

views.label: To model that dependence on God is a discipline, not a one-time decision
views.case: Even after a decisive victory, David does not coast on his prior success or on having heard from God once; he returns to ask again, teaching that faithful dependence is a repeated posture of the heart rather than information banked and reused.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge on the same lesson: David refuses to let a previous answer from God substitute for present inquiry, modeling ongoing rather than one-time dependence.

debates.question: Does David's polygamy in verse 3 receive the narrator's approval?

views.label: A neutral historical note within the honor of the moment
views.case: The verse simply records what happened as part of David's growing household and dynasty, in keeping with the royal customs of the era, without explicit narratorial judgment either way.

views.label: A quiet foreshadowing of trouble, in tension with Deuteronomy's warning
views.case: Deuteronomy 17:17 explicitly warns that the king 'must not take many wives,' and Chronicles elsewhere shows the painful fruit of David's household complications; even without an explicit rebuke here, an alert reader of the Law would sense the warning signs.

debates.bearing: The Chronicler's focus in this chapter is David's dependence on God in battle, not a verdict on his domestic life, but the wider canon does not let readers treat verse 3 as simply commendable.

study.originalLanguages: God 'highly exalted,' nasa, David's kingdom, using the very verb that will describe the Levites lifting the ark on their shoulders two chapters later, a hint that whatever is rightly lifted up, whether a king or the ark itself, is lifted by God's own appointment and authorization. And twice David darash, inquires of, God before battle, deliberately answering the charge laid against Saul, who 'did not inquire of the LORD,' 10:14; the repetition of the verb across just a few verses drives home that this is the defining difference between the two kings.

typology.type: David inquiring of God before every battle
typology.fulfillment: Christ's total, unbroken dependence on the Father, doing nothing of Himself and seeking only the Father's will, John 5:19, 30.
God highly exalting Jesus and giving Him the name above every name, Philippians 2:9-11.

study.christ: David's repeated pattern, 'David inquired of God' (verse 10), 'David again inquired of God' (verse 14), models the humble dependence that finds its perfect fulfillment in Jesus, who declared He could do nothing on His own and did not seek His own will but the will of the Father who sent Him, John 5:19, 30. The kingdom that God 'highly exalted' for David's sake, verse 2, anticipates the far greater exaltation of Christ, whom God has highly exalted to the place above every name, Philippians 2:9, the King whose enemies are subdued not by cart or by cunning but by the Father's own hand.
study.god: God is the true builder and exalter of David's house, working even through a foreign king's gift of cedar and craftsmen. He is not distant or generic in His guidance; He answers David's specific inquiries with specific instructions, down to listening for marching in treetops, showing a God who is intimately engaged in the practical details of His people's lives. His stated purpose is 'for the sake of His people Israel,' making even David's exaltation an act of covenant love toward the whole nation.

crossReferences.note: Saul died for his unfaithfulness and because he did not inquire of the LORD, the exact failure this chapter shows David repeatedly avoiding.
The parallel account of these same two Philistine victories, recorded in nearly identical language.
The law of the king, including the warning against multiplying wives, relevant background to verse 3.
Jesus' own total dependence on the Father, doing nothing of Himself, the fulfillment of the pattern David models imperfectly here.
God highly exalting Jesus and giving Him the name above every name, echoing the exaltation language of verse 2.

study.oneStory: Framed between the ark's failed and corrected journeys, this chapter shows God building David's house, growing his family, and exalting his kingdom, while David himself models the humble, repeated practice of inquiring of God rather than presuming on his own strength, standing in sharp relief against Saul's fatal self-reliance. It points every reader, and especially the returned exiles rebuilding a kingdom from nothing, toward the true King who never acted apart from His Father and whom God has now exalted above every name.

quiz.q: What does Hiram's gift of cedar and craftsmen signal about David's kingdom?

options.text: That the LORD had established David as king and highly exalted his kingdom, recognized now even by a foreign ruler.
options.note: Verses 1-2 tie Hiram's gift directly to David's realization that the LORD had established and exalted him.

options.text: That David had formed a military alliance to attack the Philistines.
options.note: Hiram's gift concerned building materials and craftsmen for a palace, not a military alliance against the Philistines.

options.text: That David was now wealthy enough to build his own temple.
options.note: The gift built David's palace, not a temple; the temple's construction belongs to Solomon later in the book.

options.text: That Tyre wanted to establish trade routes through Jerusalem.
options.note: Trade and international relations were surely part of the picture, but the text's explicit point is David's recognition that the LORD had established and exalted his kingdom.

quiz.q: What did David do differently from Saul when the Philistines attacked?

options.text: He inquired of God directly before going to battle, both times, rather than acting on assumption or past experience.
options.note: Verses 10 and 14 record David inquiring of God, contrasted with Saul's failure to inquire, 10:14.

options.text: He hired foreign mercenaries to strengthen his army.
options.note: No such hiring is recorded; the contrast the text draws is about inquiring of God, not military recruitment.

options.text: He waited for the Philistines to retreat on their own before engaging.
options.note: David actively went out to face them and later attacked; he did not simply wait for withdrawal.

options.text: He consulted his military commanders before acting.
options.note: David does confer with leaders in the surrounding narrative (13:1), but in this specific chapter the explicit contrast with Saul is about inquiring of God, not human commanders.

quiz.q: How did God answer David's second inquiry differently from the first?

options.text: God gave a detailed tactical plan, circling around to attack from the balsam trees and waiting for the sound of marching in the treetops, rather than a straightforward yes to advance directly.
options.note: Verses 14-15 record this specific, situational guidance, different from the direct 'Go' of verse 10.

options.text: God refused to answer the second time, leaving David to decide alone.
options.note: God answers clearly both times; the second answer includes detailed tactical instructions.

options.text: God told David to retreat and avoid the Philistines entirely.
options.note: God commanded engagement, not retreat, giving specific instructions for how to attack.

options.text: God simply repeated the same instruction as the first battle.
options.note: This understates the difference; the second answer was tactically distinct, requiring a flanking maneuver rather than a direct advance.

quiz.q: What happened to the Philistines' gods after the first battle at Baal-perazim?

options.text: The Philistines abandoned them there, and David ordered them burned in the fire.
options.note: Verse 12 records both the abandonment and David's command to burn them, in obedience to the Law's demand regarding idols, Deuteronomy 7:5, 25.

options.text: David kept them as trophies in his palace.
options.note: The opposite is recorded; David had them destroyed by fire, not kept as trophies.

options.text: The Philistines took their gods with them in retreat.
options.note: The text specifically says the gods were abandoned there, not carried off in retreat.

options.text: The idols were left where they fell without further comment.
options.note: This omits David's explicit command that they be burned, an act of obedience to the Law's demand to destroy idols.

quiz.q: What was the ultimate result of David's two victories over the Philistines?

options.text: David's fame spread into every land, and the LORD caused all nations to fear him.
options.note: Verse 17 records this outcome directly, framing the victories as building David's international reputation under God's hand.

options.text: The Philistines were permanently destroyed as a nation.
options.note: The Philistines remain a recurring threat throughout David's reign and beyond; they were defeated in battle here, not eliminated as a people.

options.text: David was forced to pay tribute to the Philistines afterward.
options.note: The opposite occurred; David defeated the Philistines and gained fame, not the reverse.

options.text: David's kingdom gained territory but no lasting reputation among the nations.
options.note: This understates the outcome; the text specifically highlights the spread of David's fame and the nations' fear of him, not merely territorial gain.

questions: Twice David stopped to inquire of God rather than assuming he knew what to do based on his first victory. Where in your life do you tend to rely on past experience rather than seeking God fresh for what is in front of you now?
God gave David a different, more detailed battle plan the second time. How do you respond when God's guidance for a new situation looks different from how He led you before?
The chapter contrasts David's dependence with Saul's failure to inquire of the LORD. What would it look like this week to build the simple habit of asking God before acting, rather than after?

David gave God credit, even naming the battlefield after what God had done, 'God has burst out against my enemies.' Where could you be more deliberate in giving God the credit for victories in your own life?

God's stated purpose in exalting David was 'for the sake of His people Israel,' not David's personal glory. How does remembering that your own gifts and successes are meant to serve others shape the way you use them?

translationNotes.note: Shammua is a BSB-noted variant of Shimea, 1 Chronicles 3:5; the same son appears under both names in Chronicles' genealogies.
Beeliada is a BSB-noted variant of Eliada, the same son named in the parallel list, 2 Samuel 5:16.
Baal-perazim means 'the LORD bursts out,' per the BSB footnote, David's own naming of the site after God's decisive victory there.
The BSB footnote allows 'balsam trees' to be rendered aspen or poplar trees; the identity of the tree is uncertain, but the sense of the tactical instruction is unaffected.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Chronicles 14:1-17. No dashes were present in the source text for this chapter; none appear in this file. Sermon search covered Alistair Begg, Tim Keller, John Piper, Sinclair Ferguson, and D.A. Carson for treatments specifically on 1 Chronicles 14; no verified sermon by any of the five was found, so sermon status is none.
